text.title: Content Audit — 6-Step Process
text.summary: Structured procedure for inventorying, evaluating, and assigning actions to all content assets.

section.title: Step 1: Define Scope and Goals

rule.statement: Define what content is in scope and what question the audit must answer before gathering any data.
rule.rationale: Auditing everything without a purpose produces data nobody acts on. Scoping first constrains tool selection, evaluation criteria, and report format.

section.p: Questions to answer: What content is in scope (all pages, blog only, help center)? What do we want to learn (what exists, what performs)? How will results be used (migration, strategy, cleanup)?
Evaluation criteria to set: accuracy, relevance, quality, performance, accessibility, SEO.

section.title: Step 2: Gather Content Inventory

rule.statement: Use a site crawler for automated inventory; supplement manually for gated, dynamic, and legacy content.
rule.rationale: Crawlers miss PDFs, gated content, and content served by JavaScript. A combined approach ensures completeness.

section.p: Automated: site crawler (Screaming Frog, Sitebulb), CMS export, sitemap.
Manual additions: PDFs and downloads, gated content, dynamic content, legacy systems.

section.title: Step 3: Create Spreadsheet Structure

rule.statement: Capture at minimum: URL, title, type, date, author, status, metrics, quality score, and assigned action.
rule.rationale: Consistent columns allow sorting by action and filtering by priority. Missing columns force a second pass.

section.reference: Column schema for the content inventory spreadsheet

section.title: Step 4: Evaluate Each Piece

rule.statement: Score each content item against all evaluation criteria before assigning an action.
rule.rationale: Skipping evaluation and jumping to action produces inconsistent decisions that stakeholders cannot trust.

section.p: Per-item questions: Is it accurate and up-to-date? Relevant to users? Well-written? Performing well in analytics? Accessible? SEO-optimized?

rule.statement: Assign exactly one of six actions to every content item: Keep, Update, Consolidate, Rewrite, Remove, or Review.
rule.rationale: Unassigned items become backlog debt. Six distinct actions prevent the common collapse into "keep or delete" which misses consolidation and rewrite opportunities.

list.item: Keep — high quality, performing well, accurate.
Update — good content, needs refresh or minor corrections.
Consolidate — duplicate or overlapping with another piece.
Rewrite — poor quality but important topic.
Remove — outdated, irrelevant, or low value with no recovery path.
Review — needs further evaluation before deciding.

section.title: Step 6: Report and Recommend

rule.statement: Summarize findings by type, age, and action; include prioritized recommendations with explicit owners and timelines.
rule.rationale: A report without owners and timelines produces no action. Priority tiers (immediate/short-term/long-term) make the workload plannable.

section.reference: Executive summary and findings report skeleton
